title: A Helping Hand
description: A friendly local notices your bike chain has slipped. They stop and offer to help fix it before you even realize there's a problem.

options.resultText: They fix your chain in minutes and wave you off with a smile. How Danish of them!
options.culturalTip: 💡 Danes often help strangers with practical matters without expecting anything in return.

options.text: Try to fix it yourself first
options.resultText: You manage it yourself. The local nods approvingly — self-reliance is respected here.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Cycling is practically a life skill in Denmark. Most Danes learn basic bike maintenance early.

title: Free Danish Lessons
description: A notice board at the community centre advertises free Danish language classes starting this week. A volunteer coordinator is standing nearby handing out flyers.

options.resultText: You sign up! The coordinator beams and hands you a schedule. Your Danish journey is about to accelerate.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Denmark offers free or subsidised Danish classes (Danskuddannelse) to immigrants. Take advantage!

options.text: Take a flyer but commit later
options.resultText: You pocket the flyer. Maybe next week. The opportunity remains open.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Most municipalities require immigrants to attend language courses. It's both a right and an obligation.

title: Shared Shelter
description: A sudden downpour catches you unprepared. A woman at the bus stop smiles and gestures for you to share her umbrella.

options.resultText: You chat while waiting for the bus. She turns out to be a librarian with tips on settling in.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Despite the reserved reputation, Danes can be very warm in small moments of shared adversity.

options.text: Politely decline and dash for cover
options.resultText: You make it to a shop awning, soaked but smiling. At least the rain is refreshing.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Denmark averages about 170 rainy days per year. An umbrella is a year-round investment.

title: Gateway to Knowledge
description: A librarian notices you looking overwhelmed by the Danish interface on the public computer. She offers to help you sign up for a library card.

options.text: Accept her help and get a card
options.resultText: With your library card you can borrow books, use computers, and attend events for free. A true treasure.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Danish public libraries (Folkebiblioteket) offer free access to books, films, music, and even tools.

options.text: Try to navigate the system yourself
options.resultText: You manage to navigate the Danish forms yourself. The librarian gives you an impressed nod.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Libraries in Denmark often provide settlement guidance and host integration events.

title: A Tip from a Stranger
description: You overhear two people at a café discussing job openings in your field. One of them notices your interest and introduces themselves.

options.resultText: Your Danish surprises them. They share contacts and suggest applying this week. Networking Danish-style!
options.culturalTip: 💡 Much of the Danish job market runs on informal networks. Coffee meetings are a normal part of job hunting.

options.resultText: You catch snippets of useful information but miss the networking opportunity.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Even a brief introduction can open doors in Denmark's relatively flat, egalitarian workplace culture.

title: Insider Knowledge
description: A friendly cashier at the grocery store points out a yellow discount sticker on items that expire today — up to 50% off.

options.resultText: You save 30 DKK and get a week's worth of meals. The yellow sticker is your new best friend.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Danskerne (Danes) are savvy shoppers. Yellow 'tilbud' (offer) stickers near closing time are golden.

options.text: Pick just one or two items
options.resultText: You grab a couple of bargains and head home with extra change in your pocket.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Food waste is a serious concern in Denmark. Discounted near-expiry food is a win for your wallet and the planet.

title: Savvy Commuter Advice
description: A commuter sees you fumbling with the metro ticket machine and explains that a monthly pass is cheaper than daily tickets if you ride more than 10 times.

options.text: Ask more about the pass options
options.resultText: You learn about zones, monthly passes, and the Rejsekort. Your commute is about to get cheaper.
options.culturalTip: 💡 The Rejsekort is Denmark's contactless smart card for all public transport, offering automatic discounts.

options.resultText: You note the tip mentally. The monthly pass question stays in the back of your mind.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Copenhagen's metro runs 24/7, making it one of the most convenient city transit systems in the world.

title: A Recipe for Integration
description: Your neighbour smells your cooking and knocks to ask what you're making. The conversation turns to Danish cuisine and they offer to teach you their grandmother's recipe for smørrebrød.

options.resultText: You spend a delightful hour learning about Danish open-faced sandwiches. Culture through cuisine!
options.culturalTip: 💡 Smørrebrød (open-faced rye bread sandwiches) is a cornerstone of Danish food culture and social life.

options.resultText: Your neighbour understands and leaves a handwritten recipe card. How thoughtful.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Rye bread (rugbrød) is eaten daily by most Danes. It's dense, nutritious, and an acquired taste.

title: Festival Invitation
description: A colleague at work mentions a free cultural festival in the park this weekend. They invite you to join their group.

options.resultText: The festival is wonderful — music, hygge, and new friends. You feel more at home in Denmark.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Danes are festival lovers. From Roskilde to local neighbourhood fests, summer is full of free events.

options.text: Decline but ask about future events
options.resultText: They share a calendar of upcoming events. Baby steps toward social integration.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Cultural participation is an important part of integration recognised by Danish authorities.

title: Neighbourhood Workshop
description: You spot a sign for a free community bike repair workshop in the neighbourhood. Volunteers help fix bikes every Saturday morning.

options.text: Bring your bike in for maintenance
options.resultText: The volunteers tune up your bike perfectly. They also teach you how to do it yourself next time.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Community repair cafés are common in Denmark, reflecting the culture of sustainability and self-reliance.

options.resultText: You pick up some useful tips by watching the volunteers work.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Over 62% of Danes cycle to work or school. Keeping your bike maintained is essential.

title: A New Connection
description: Lars introduces you to his friend Thomas, a community organiser who knows everyone in the neighbourhood. He could be a valuable contact.

options.resultText: Thomas is immediately warm and says you should come to the next neighbourhood meeting.
options.culturalTip: 💡 In Denmark, community involvement (foreningsliv) is central to social life. Joining groups opens many doors.

options.resultText: You exchange numbers. Thomas seems like someone worth knowing better.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Danes may seem reserved at first but become very loyal once they consider you part of their circle.

title: Navigating the System
description: An expat at the community centre shares a document checklist they wish they'd had when first arrived. It covers CPR numbers, Nem ID, and bank accounts.

options.text: Sit down and go through the list together
options.resultText: An hour later you have a clear roadmap for your first month of Danish bureaucracy. Invaluable!
options.culturalTip: 💡 Getting your CPR number (civil registration) is the first crucial step — almost nothing works without it.

options.resultText: You tuck the list away. Even a quick scan reveals several things you hadn't considered.
options.culturalTip: 💡 NemID/MitID is Denmark's digital signature system, required for online banking, government forms, and more.

title: Picnic Invitation
description: A group of mixed nationalities are having a picnic in the park and wave you over. There's food, music, and laughter.

options.resultText: The afternoon flies by in a blur of conversation, food, and sunshine. Copenhagen at its best.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Danes love outdoor socialising in summer. Parks fill up at the first ray of sunshine.

options.resultText: You enjoy the lively atmosphere as you pass by. Next time, maybe.
options.culturalTip: 💡 The Danish concept of 'hygge' (cosiness and warmth) thrives in outdoor gatherings like these.

title: Gløgg and Goodwill
description: A neighbourhood stall is giving out free cups of warm gløgg (mulled wine) on this cold winter day. The vendor is cheerful and chatty.

options.resultText: The warm gløgg and good conversation lift your spirits on a dark December day. Pure hygge.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Gløgg (Danish mulled wine) is a winter staple. Danish Christmas (Jul) is characterised by warmth and togetherness.

options.resultText: You wrap your hands around the warm cup and walk on. A small but appreciated kindness.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Danes cope with dark winters through 'lystækning' (candles) and cosy social gatherings — hygge in action.

title: Frokost Invitation
description: A colleague invites you to join the team for the traditional Danish lunch (frokost). It's a daily ritual you've been missing out on.

options.resultText: The team lunch is warm and informal. You learn more in an hour than in weeks of observation.
options.culturalTip: 💡 The Danish frokost (lunch) is a social institution. Being invited means you're being included in the team.

options.resultText: Even a brief appearance is appreciated. You're slowly becoming part of the team.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Danish work culture values participation in shared rituals. Opting out regularly can signal you're not integrated.

title: Street Serenade
description: A street musician is playing a beautiful folk melody near the pedestrian zone. Passersby slow down to listen.

options.text: Stop and listen for a while
options.resultText: The music transports you. You recognize a melody you've heard before. Copenhagen has a soundtrack.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Danish folk music (spillemandsmusik) has deep roots. Street performance is protected and celebrated.

options.text: Drop a coin and walk on
options.resultText: A small gesture for a moment of beauty. The musician nods gratefully.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Street musicians in Copenhagen must have a permit. Many are trained professionals.

title: Danish Weather Talk
description: At the bus stop, an older man strikes up a conversation about the unpredictable Danish weather. He seems to enjoy the complaint as much as the weather itself.

options.resultText: You stumble through the conversation but he appreciates the effort. Small talk is its own skill.
options.culturalTip: 💡 The Danish word 'vejr' (weather) is the most popular small-talk topic. Master it and you can talk to anyone.

options.resultText: The man rambles happily. You understand about 30% but the warmth of the exchange is real.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Denmark gets only about 1700 sunshine hours per year — less than Southern Europe — so weather is always topical.

title: Lost Tourist
description: A confused tourist approaches with a map and a bewildered expression, asking for directions to Nyhavn in broken English.

options.resultText: You point them toward Nyhavn and feel a small surge of pride. You know your city now.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Nyhavn (New Harbour) is Copenhagen's iconic 17th-century waterfront. Hans Christian Andersen lived there.

options.text: Apologise and say you're new too
options.resultText: You share a moment of lost-in-translation solidarity. They pull out Google Maps. Crisis averted.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Copenhagen welcomes over 3 million tourists per year. Your city is a global destination.

title: Autumn Colours
description: The park is ablaze with autumn colours. Children are kicking through leaf piles. A photographer is capturing the scene.

options.resultText: The colours and cool air invigorate you. Denmark in autumn has a melancholy beauty all its own.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Autumn in Denmark is celebrated for cosy (hygge) evenings, harvest markets, and dramatic skies.

options.resultText: You catch glimpses of golden trees as you pass. Even in a hurry, beauty finds you.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Danes are great walkers — even in cold weather — and urban parks are central to city life.

title: By the Canal
description: You find yourself by one of Copenhagen's beautiful canals. Boats rock gently. An old man feeds ducks from a bench.

options.resultText: The gentle sound of water and distant city hum creates a moment of perfect calm.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Copenhagen has over 60 km of canals. The harbour is clean enough to swim in during summer.

options.text: Take a few photos and continue
options.resultText: You capture the scene on your phone. This city has a way of surprising you with beauty.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Nyhavn and the canals are among the most photographed spots in all of Scandinavia.

title: Rush Hour
description: The metro is packed during rush hour. People are quiet, respectful of personal space, looking at their phones. The Danish way.

options.resultText: You notice how everyone maintains space and quiet — a shared unspoken agreement. Fascinating.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Danes are quiet on public transport. Making eye contact or speaking loudly can feel intrusive to locals.

options.text: Listen to music and zone out
options.resultText: You arrive at your stop refreshed. Sometimes a commute is just a commute.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Copenhagen's metro is driverless and runs every 3-4 minutes during peak hours.

title: Furry Ambassador
description: A large, friendly dog bounds over to you in the park, tail wagging furiously. The owner calls out 'Han bider ikke!' (He doesn't bite!)

options.text: Pet the dog and chat with the owner
options.resultText: A brief, warm interaction. Dog owners are an easy social bridge in any city.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Danes are very fond of their dogs. Copenhagen is considered one of the most dog-friendly cities in Europe.

options.resultText: The owner calls the dog back. No harm done — it was a happy moment regardless.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Most Danish parks are dog-friendly. You'll often see dogs unleashed in designated areas.

title: Festival Atmosphere
description: The neighbourhood is buzzing. A summer street festival has closed a road nearby — there's music, food stalls, and people everywhere.

options.resultText: The energy is infectious. Street food, live music, and strangers becoming temporary friends.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Denmark has over 100 music and culture festivals each summer. Participation is seen as a civic duty of joy.

options.resultText: The colour and noise wash over you pleasantly. You can still feel the atmosphere without diving in.
options.culturalTip: 💡 The Roskilde Festival is one of Europe's largest music events, attracting 130,000 visitors annually.

title: City Under Construction
description: A bike path is blocked by construction. Workers in orange vests direct you around. The city is always being improved.

options.resultText: The detour takes you through a street you've never seen. Every obstacle hides a discovery.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Copenhagen has invested billions in expanding cycling infrastructure. Temporary disruptions are a sign of progress.

options.text: Ask a worker for the quickest route
options.resultText: The worker gives you a shortcut with hand signals and a smile. Mission accomplished.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Danish construction workers are among the best-paid and most regulated in Europe, thanks to strong unions.

title: Eavesdropping for Education
description: You overhear an animated conversation between two elderly women at a café. You understand more than you expected.

options.text: Listen carefully and try to understand
options.resultText: You catch the gist: gardening gossip and TV recommendations. Progress is progress!
options.culturalTip: 💡 Passive listening to natural conversation is one of the most effective ways to improve language comprehension.

options.text: Tune out and focus on your coffee
options.resultText: The rhythm of Danish washes over you gently. Even passive exposure helps.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Danish uses a 'stød' — a unique glottal stop — that doesn't exist in many other languages. It takes time.

title: First Flowers of Spring
description: The first crocuses of spring are peeking through the soil in the park. An elderly gardener is tending to a flower bed with evident pride.

options.resultText: The gardener lights up. You exchange a few halting words about spring. Joy needs no translation.
options.culturalTip: 💡 The Danish love of gardening is legendary. Community allotment gardens (kolonihaver) are cherished across the country.

options.resultText: After a long winter, those tiny flowers feel like a victory. Spring always returns.
options.culturalTip: 💡 After months of darkness, the first days of spring are genuinely celebrated in Denmark — often called 'Forårsfornemmelse'.

title: The Bell Warning
description: You accidentally drift into the wrong bike lane. A cyclist behind you rings their bell urgently.

options.resultText: You wave apologetically. The cyclist nods and speeds past. Lesson learned.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Bike lane etiquette in Copenhagen is serious. Stay right, signal turns, and never stop in the lane.

options.resultText: You watch the lanes carefully before rejoining. Traffic flows around you patiently.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Copenhagen has a strict hierarchy: pedestrians walk, cyclists cycle in their lane, and cars yield to both.

title: Urban Canvas
description: An entire building wall has been transformed into a stunning mural. A small group of people photograph it.

options.resultText: The mural depicts Danish folk tales. The city speaks in many languages.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Nørrebro and Vesterbro are Copenhagen's most vibrant neighbourhoods for street art and alternative culture.

options.text: Walk past with a quick glance
options.resultText: The colours catch your eye briefly. The city is its own gallery.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Copenhagen has designated legal graffiti walls where artists can freely express themselves.

title: The Long Dark
description: It's 4pm and already dark. You find yourself affected by the short days more than you expected. Others seem to cope with candles and warm lights.

options.text: Lean into the hygge — light some candles
options.resultText: Warm light, a hot drink, and soft music. You understand hygge now — it's a survival mechanism.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Denmark uses more candles per capita than any other country in the world. It's a literal coping mechanism.

options.text: Push through with extra vitamin D
options.resultText: Practical and effective. Winter has biological strategies and psychological ones — you're learning both.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Vitamin D deficiency affects many people in Scandinavia. Supplementation is common and recommended.

title: Weekend Market
description: A weekend market has appeared in the square — organic produce, crafts, and the smell of freshly baked bread.

options.text: Browse the stalls and buy something
options.resultText: You pick up fresh vegetables and a jar of local honey. Worth every krone.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Organic food markets (Økomarked) are very popular in Denmark, which has the world's highest per capita organic food consumption.

options.resultText: Window shopping is also participation. You make note of the next market day.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Saturday markets are a weekend ritual for many Danish families. Early morning is best for quality produce.

title: Headlines in Danish
description: A free newspaper on the bench has headlines you half-understand. Something about a political debate and something about football.

options.resultText: You understand more than yesterday. The struggle with the language is real but progress is measurable.
options.culturalTip: 💡 'MetroXpress' and 'Urban' are Denmark's major free daily papers. Great for language practice commuting.

options.text: Scan the headlines and put it down
options.resultText: Something about taxes and something about handball. Close enough.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Denmark is consistently rated among the world's top countries for press freedom.

title: The Danish Queue
description: There's a long, orderly queue outside a government office. Everyone waits patiently with their number ticket.

options.resultText: Forty minutes later you're seen. The efficiency is impressive once it's your turn.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Danes take queuing very seriously. Jumping the queue is a social taboo of the highest order.

options.text: Use the app to book an appointment instead
options.resultText: You book an appointment for tomorrow and walk away. Digital Denmark rewards the prepared.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Most Danish government services have an online booking option. Borger.dk is the central government portal.

title: Park Life
description: The local park is alive with activity — families, joggers, cyclists, and chess players. Life in Copenhagen moves through green spaces.

options.text: Find a bench and watch the world go by
options.resultText: An hour passes like minutes. This park is a microcosm of Danish society.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Fælledparken in Copenhagen is the largest park and hosts everything from concerts to football to casual picnics.

options.text: Join a game of frisbee nearby
options.resultText: The group welcomes you without hesitation. Sport is a universal language.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Danes have a strong tradition of organised sports clubs (idrætsforeninger) but also love informal outdoor play.

title: Caught in the Rain
description: A sudden downpour catches you completely unprepared — no jacket, no umbrella. You're getting soaked.

options.text: Make a run for the nearest shop
options.resultText: You buy a cheap umbrella. Never again without one in Copenhagen.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Always carry a light jacket in Copenhagen — the weather changes quickly. Locals say there's no bad weather, only bad clothing.

options.text: Duck into a café and wait it out
options.resultText: Forced café break turns into a pleasant hour. The rain stops eventually.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Danish cafés are warm, cosy refuges. 'Kaffetæring' (needing coffee) is an accepted reason to be anywhere.

title: Wrong Direction
description: You board the metro and realise after two stops that you're heading the wrong way. The map looked the same both ways!

options.text: Get off at the next stop and cross over
options.resultText: You figure it out and arrive only slightly late. Every mistake teaches the city.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Copenhagen's metro has two main lines (M1 and M2) plus newer additions. Study the map carefully.

options.text: Ask a fellow passenger for help
options.resultText: The passenger switches to English immediately and guides you. Danish directness is actually very helpful.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Danes are among the world's best English speakers. Asking for help will almost always work.

title: Lost in Translation
description: A government official is explaining something important to you entirely in Danish, despite your confused expression. They don't seem to notice.

options.text: Politely ask if they speak English
options.resultText: They switch to perfect English immediately. You feel relief and slight embarrassment in equal measure.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Almost all Danish officials speak excellent English. Don't be afraid to ask — it's not impolite.

options.text: Nod along and figure it out later
options.resultText: You catch about half of it but miss something important. You'll need to follow up.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Danish bureaucracy runs on specific documents and deadlines. When in doubt, always ask for clarification.

title: Flat Tyre Misfortune
description: Your bike tyre hisses and goes flat halfway to your destination. You're going to be late.

options.text: Fix it yourself with your repair kit
options.resultText: Fifteen minutes later you're back on the road. That cycling skill is paying off!
options.culturalTip: 💡 Most Danish cyclists carry a basic repair kit. Punctures are common on city streets.

options.text: Lock up the bike and take the bus
options.resultText: You arrive late but unruffled. The bike can wait until after work.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Copenhagen has an integrated transport system — switching between bike, bus and metro is seamless.

title: The Mysterious Form
description: You've received an official-looking letter in Danish. It contains a form you must complete and return within 14 days. The consequences of not doing so are unclear.

options.text: Translate it yourself and fill it in
options.resultText: It was a routine address registration update. Your growing Danish skills saved the day.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Denmark relies heavily on digital post (e-Boks). All official correspondence goes there. Register early!

options.text: Ask a Danish-speaking friend to help
options.resultText: Lars helps you decode it in minutes. It was a census form — filled in correctly now.
options.culturalTip: 💡 e-Boks.dk is the secure digital mailbox used by all Danish authorities. You must register when you get your CPR number.

title: A Difficult Neighbour
description: Your neighbour knocks angrily on your door, saying your music is too loud at 9pm. They seem very upset.

options.text: Apologise sincerely and turn it down
options.resultText: They soften slightly. You learn that 10pm is the hard cut-off for noise in Danish apartments.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Most Danish apartment rules (husordensregler) prohibit loud noise after 10pm. Respect this — it's taken seriously.

options.resultText: You stand your ground respectfully. The neighbour is surprised but somewhat pacified. Conflict in Danish!
options.culturalTip: 💡 Direct communication is valued in Denmark. Explaining your side calmly is always appropriate.

title: Last Metro Gone
description: You've missed the last S-tog of the night. Your phone is almost dead and you're far from home.

options.text: Call a taxi — worth the expense
options.resultText: Expensive but safe. Note to self: the Cityringen metro runs 24/7 — use it next time.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Copenhagen's Cityringen metro line runs 24/7. S-tog and buses have limited night service but Night Buses (N) run at weekends.

options.text: Cycle home — it's not that far
options.resultText: Night cycling in Copenhagen is safe and actually beautiful. You arrive home invigorated.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Copenhagen is ranked one of the safest cities in the world for night cycling. Well-lit paths are everywhere.

title: Tax Season
description: It's April and you've received your annual tax assessment (årsopgørelse) from SKAT. The numbers don't look right.

options.resultText: You correct an error in your deductions and get a refund! Mastering bureaucracy has financial rewards.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Most Danish employees get a tax refund in April. The system is pre-filled — you just need to check and approve.

options.text: Ask a professional to review it
options.resultText: A small fee, but peace of mind. Your taxes are correct and submitted on time.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Denmark has one of the world's most efficient tax systems. SKAT (the tax authority) is surprisingly user-friendly online.

title: Cultural Miscalculation
description: You arrive at a dinner party 15 minutes early — in your home culture, being early is polite. Your Danish hosts look uncomfortable.

options.text: Apologise and offer to help set up
options.resultText: The hosts relax and actually appreciate the help. You learn a crucial cultural lesson.
options.culturalTip: 💡 In Denmark, punctuality means arriving exactly on time — not early. Being 15 minutes early is as awkward as being late.

options.text: Pretend you were just checking the address
options.resultText: A white lie saves the moment. You walk around the block for 15 minutes before ringing the bell.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Danish social invitations come with implicit rules. 'Come for dinner at 7' means arrive at exactly 7:00.

title: Missing Wallet Panic
description: You reach for your wallet and it's not there. Did you leave it at home? Drop it? Have you been pickpocketed?

options.resultText: Found it! It slipped under the café seat. Heart rate normalising.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Denmark is one of the world's safest countries. Wallets are frequently returned intact — theft is genuinely rare.

options.text: Report it to police just in case
options.resultText: The police are professional and helpful. Two hours later someone calls — they found it at the library.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Danes return found valuables. A wallet left on a park bench in Copenhagen has a very good chance of being returned.

title: Danish Prøve
description: You receive notice that you must take a Danish language test (Prøve i Dansk) as part of your integration programme within 30 days.

options.resultText: You pass with distinction! The certificate opens new doors for permanent residency and better jobs.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Prøve i Dansk (PD) has three levels. PD3 is required for permanent residency applications in Denmark.

options.text: Study what you can in the time available
options.resultText: You pass at the basic level. It counts, and you can retake it. Every step counts.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Free Danish lessons (Danskuddannelse) are required for most immigrants and are provided by municipalities.

title: Bare Shelves
description: The grocery store is sold out of everything you needed for dinner. A local holiday you weren't aware of closed most shops for a day.

options.resultText: The kiosk prices sting but you eat. You add Danish holidays to your calendar immediately.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Denmark has many public holidays (helligdage). Most shops close entirely — learn the calendar early.

options.text: Make do with what's at home
options.resultText: Creative cooking from pantry staples. Sometimes constraints breed culinary invention.
options.culturalTip: 💡 7-Eleven and DSB kiosks (train station shops) are among the few places open on Danish public holidays.

title: The Five-Year Milestone
description: A letter from Udlændingestyrelsen (the Immigration Service) details the requirements for permanent residency. After five years, you might qualify. This changes everything.

options.resultText: The process is complex but you've come so far. This is what all the preparation was for.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Permanent residency in Denmark requires 5+ years of residence, language skills, employment, and community ties.

options.resultText: Knowledge is preparation. You now have a clear roadmap to the permanent residency milestone.
options.culturalTip: 💡 The Danish integration tests include points for employment, language, active citizenship, and self-sufficiency.

title: Something Clicked
description: After months in Denmark, you wake up one morning and realise that something has fundamentally shifted. You dreamed in Danish. The city feels like home.

options.resultText: You are not just surviving Denmark. You are living it. This is the real integration milestone.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Cultural integration is not just about paperwork. The moment Denmark feels like home is when you truly arrive.

options.text: Write in your journal about this moment
options.resultText: You document the feeling carefully. The journey will make for a remarkable story.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Many immigrants describe a specific moment when Denmark stopped being foreign. Treasure yours.

title: Asked to Lead
description: Thomas approaches you with an unexpected proposal: help organise the neighbourhood integration committee. People trust you now. This is a turning point.

options.resultText: You step into the role. From immigrant to leader — this is the story Denmark makes possible.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Foreningsliv (association life) is central to Danish democracy. Volunteering gives you real belonging and civic rights.

options.text: Say you need time to think
options.resultText: Thomas understands. He leaves the offer open. Some decisions deserve deliberation.
options.culturalTip: 💡 The Danish volunteer sector employs the equivalent of 50,000 full-time positions. Community involvement is deeply valued.

title: Hygge Embodied
description: You pass a lit window — inside, friends sit around candles, laughing and talking. For a moment you see exactly what the word 'hygge' means.

options.resultText: Hygge isn't a concept — it's a feeling. You just felt it. You're closer to Denmark than you realise.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Hygge defies simple translation. It's warmth, togetherness, and contentment — a Danish philosophy of everyday life.

options.text: Feel the warmth and walk on
options.resultText: The warmth stays with you on the walk home. Community is everywhere if you look.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Denmark consistently ranks as one of the happiest countries in the world, largely attributed to its culture of hygge.

title: Soaking Up the Sun
description: On a rare sunny day in November, colleagues are rushing outside to sit in the weak sunlight during their break. They insist you join.

options.resultText: The fifteen minutes of pale November sunshine feels like gold. You understand the Danish sun worship now.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Danes abandon offices at any hint of sunshine in autumn. Even weak sun is actively sought and celebrated.

options.text: Stay at your desk but wave
options.resultText: Your colleagues wave back. You miss the warmth but finish your task. Sometimes duty calls.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Vitamin D deficiency is common in Nordic countries. Sun breaks are genuinely health-motivated, not just pleasant.

title: Ice on the Path
description: The bike path is icy and treacherous after last night's freeze. Other cyclists are navigating carefully. Do you dare?

options.resultText: You make it without incident. Winter cycling is its own art form — now you're practising it.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Over 20% of Copenhageners cycle in winter. All-weather cycling is a point of civic pride.

options.text: Play it safe and take the bus
options.resultText: Smart choice. Winter cycling requires special tyres and experience. You live to ride another day.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Winter cycling in Copenhagen requires studded tyres, reflective gear, and confident lane positioning.

title: Neighbourhood Open House
description: A 'Åbent Hus' (Open House) sign is posted at the community centre. Free entry, free coffee, various local organisations showing their work.

options.text: Pop in and see what's there
options.resultText: You discover three new organisations to follow and get three new email subscriptions. Danish networking at its best.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Community centres (kulturhuse) offer everything from language cafés to yoga to political meetings — and it's usually free.

options.resultText: You note the centre's address. Maybe next time.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Getting involved in local community life is one of the fastest paths to social integration in Denmark.

title: Sprogcafé
description: A language café (sprogcafé) at the library pairs new residents with Danish speakers for weekly conversation practice. The coordinator spots you browsing and invites you.

options.text: Join the café starting this week
options.resultText: Your Danish partner is patient and encouraging. An hour of real conversation is worth a week of classes.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Language cafés are free and run by volunteers in most Danish municipalities. They're social as much as educational.

options.resultText: The flyer goes on the fridge. A good intention waiting to become reality.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Regular conversation practice is the single most important factor in language acquisition, say linguists.

title: Fodbold Fever
description: Denmark has just won an important football match and the city is buzzing. Strangers are high-fiving in the street.

options.resultText: You cheer with strangers and receive a Danish flag waved in your direction. Sport brings people together.
options.culturalTip: 💡 Denmark's 1992 European Championship win (Europamesterskabet) remains a defining national moment. Football is a religion here.

options.resultText: The collective joy is contagious even from the sidelines. Denmark has won something.
options.culturalTip: 💡 FC Copenhagen (FCK) and Brøndby IF are the biggest clubs. The rivalry is intense but friendly.
